coredata.dc:title: Eu3+@metal–organic frameworks encapsulating carbon dots as ratiometric fluorescent probes for rapid recognition of anthrax spore biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Yi, Kuiyu
Zhang, Xiaoting
Zhang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Anthrax spores pose a serious threat to human well-being and life, so it is highly desirable to develop a rapid, sensitive, and selective quantitative assay of calcium pyridine dicarboxylate (CaDPA) as a biomarker of anthrax spores. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) chelated Eu3+@metal–organic framework (Eu-MOFs) as dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent (RF) probe was successfully fabricated by a simple one-pot in situ selective self-assembly synthetic strategy. The developed RF probe has an effective self-calibration function, which performs a highly sensitive and selective recognition of CaDPA in water and human serum sample. The blue-emitting CDs was employed as an effective fluorescence reference, while the Eu-MOFs exhibited enhanced red fluorescence signal through the coordination interaction with CaDPA chromophore. The sensing mechanism is attributed to that CaDPA can sensitize Eu3+ intrinsic luminescence due to the energy transfer from CaDPA to Eu3+. What's more interesting is that with the continuous drop of CaDPA, the emission color of CDs@Eu-MOF changes from blue to red. The results revealed that CDs@Eu-MOFs RF probe can detect CaDPA effectively in the range of 8–170 μg/L with good linear relationship, and exhibited a remarkable selectivity for CaDPA. More interestingly, a paper-based probe has also been devised for on-site detection of CaDPA. In addition, CaDPA is used as input signal to construct an IHIBITION logic gate device which performs the “off-on” mode. The constructed CDs@Eu-MOF probe can achieve exceptionally rapid, highly sensitive and selective detection of CaDPA, which can further expand the application prospects in environmental and biological analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anthrax spore biomarker
Ratiometric fluorescent
Eu-MOF cages
Test paper
IHIBITION logic gate device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of RF probe to CaDPA
Fluorescence response time of RF probe to CaDPA
CaDPA detection based on a turn on approach
Detection of CaDPA in water and human serum
Detection of CaDPA on test strips
Logic gate device based on “turn-on” behavior

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( NSFC 51672116 ), Liaoning Province Science and Technology Planning Project (No. 20180551203 ), the Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Project for Innovative Talent Training (No. LT2017010 ). The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this work.

This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51672116), Liaoning Province Science and Technology Planning Project (No. 20180551203), the Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Project for Innovative Talent Training (No. LT2017010). The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Eu-MOFs (A, B) before and (C) after addition CDs, and TEM images of (D) CDs, and (E, F) CDs@Eu-MOFs.

(A) XRD patterns, and (B) XPS spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs (curve a), Eu-MOFs (curve b), CDs (curve c), respectively; XPS high-resolution spectra of N 1 s, Eu 3d before (panels D, F) and after Eu-MOFs doping with CDs (panels C, E).

Fluorescence emission spectrum of (A) CDs@Eu MOFs, and (B) Eu MOFs before (curve a), and after the addition of 170 μg/L CaDPA (curve b), inset of (A) and (B): the photographs of before (a), and after the addition of 170 μg/L CaDPA (b) under an UV lamp; (C) effect of different concentrations of CaDPA on the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs; (D) the F616/F455 of CDs@Eu MOFs versus time in the presence of 15 μg/L (a), 80 μg/L (b), and 140 μg/L (c) CaDPA.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) CDs@Eu-MOFs, and (C) Eu-MOFs in the presence of CaDPA with different concentrations; the linear calibration plot of (B) F616/F455 (CDs@Eu-MOFs), (D) the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs versus the different concentrations of CaDPA.
Fluorescence response of the RF probes toward different potentially interferences; (B) Photographs of the RF probes upon addition different analytes under an UV lamp.

(A) Photographs of the fluorescence of paper strips sprayed with different analytes under an UV lamp (B) The color variations of paper strip under an UV lamp after writing by (a) individual CaDPA solution, (b) CaDPA with mixed amino acids, and (c) CaDPA with mixed aromatic ligands.

(A) Column diagram of the normalized fluorescence intensity of F616 and F455 toward CaDPA; (B) truth table of the logic gate device; (C) truth table of the constructed logic gate for CaDPA monitoring.
Synthetic route for CDs@Eu-MOFs and their dual-emissive RF detection toward CaDPA.
Determination of CaDPA in tap water and human serum.

head.ce:title: Eu3+@metal–organic frameworks encapsulating carbon dots as ratiometric fluorescent probes for rapid recognition of anthrax spore biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, 66 Chongshan Middle Road, Shenyang, Liaoning 110036, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, 66 Chongshan Middle Road, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110036, People'’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anthrax spores pose a serious threat to human well-being and life, so it is highly desirable to develop a rapid, sensitive, and selective quantitative assay of calcium pyridine dicarboxylate (CaDPA) as a biomarker of anthrax spores. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) chelated Eu3+@metal–organic framework (Eu-MOFs) as dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent (RF) probe was successfully fabricated by a simple one-pot in situ selective self-assembly synthetic strategy. The developed RF probe has an effective self-calibration function, which performs a highly sensitive and selective recognition of CaDPA in water and human serum sample. The blue-emitting CDs was employed as an effective fluorescence reference, while the Eu-MOFs exhibited enhanced red fluorescence signal through the coordination interaction with CaDPA chromophore. The sensing mechanism is attributed to that CaDPA can sensitize Eu3+ intrinsic luminescence due to the energy transfer from CaDPA to Eu3+. What's more interesting is that with the continuous drop of CaDPA, the emission color of CDs@Eu-MOF changes from blue to red. The results revealed that CDs@Eu-MOFs RF probe can detect CaDPA effectively in the range of 8–170 μg/L with good linear relationship, and exhibited a remarkable selectivity for CaDPA. More interestingly, a paper-based probe has also been devised for on-site detection of CaDPA. In addition, CaDPA is used as input signal to construct an IHIBITION logic gate device which performs the “off-on” mode. The constructed CDs@Eu-MOF probe can achieve exceptionally rapid, highly sensitive and selective detection of CaDPA, which can further expand the application prospects in environmental and biological analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is an acute infectious disease caused by Bacillus anthracis, which has a wide transmission route, high prevalence, and great harm (Song et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2005). At the same time, Bacillus anthracis as a potential biological warfare agent poses a serious threat to human well-being and life (Enserink, 2001; Zhang et al., 2006). Therefore, establishing a rapid and effective method for detecting Bacillus anthracis spores is of great significance for controlling the spread of anthrax and preventing terrorism. Among them, calcium pyridine dicarboxylate (CaDPA) is the main biomarker of Bacillus anthracis spores, accounting for 5–15% of the dry weight of spores (Aspholm et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2019; Setlow, 2006). Until now, a variety of traditional analytical methods have been used to effectively detect anthrax (Beverly and Voorhees, 2000; King et al., 2003; Mabry et al., 2006; Paulus, 1981; Ryu et al., 2010; Zhou et al., 2005). Although these methods have good accuracy and selectivity, it is regrettable that there are still some problems that need to be solved urgently. For example, chromatography and mass spectrometry requires complex and time-consuming sample preparation procedures and expensive instruments (Beverly and Voorhees, 2000; Paulus, 1981). Immunoassay technology requires expensive chemicals, time-consuming testing procedures, and professional operators (Mabry et al., 2006). Therefore, it is necessary to develop a novel method to detect anthrax biomarkers in water and serum samples for environmental and biological monitoring.

At present, fluorescence analysis is considered to be a powerful detection tool due to its advantages such as good selectivity, high sensitivity, non-destructive characteristics, real-time, fast response, and low cost (Chen et al., 2018; Navarro et al., 2016; Qiu et al., 2019b; Wu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015). Lanthanide-based fluorescent sensors have been regarded as an extremely fascinating tool in biological and environmental sensing due to their unique optical and spectral properties (Cui et al., 2019; Lian et al., 2017; Qin and Yan, 2018; Zhao et al., 2019). Up to date, several lanthanide-based fluorescence probes have been developed to detect Bacillus anthracis biomarkers by using various types of matrix such as gold nanoparticles (Donmez et al., 2017), silver nanoparticles (Tan et al., 2013), silica nanoparticles (Ai et al., 2009), graphene (Wang et al., 2015), polyethersulfone films (Lee et al., 2013), carbon nanotubes (Tan et al., 2011), coordination polymer (Tan et al., 2014), and metal organic frameworks (Xu et al., 2012). However, most of the reported research is based on single change of lanthanide ions fluorescence, which is easily affected by the external environment and test equipment. Yet now RF probes can effectively build-in correction for the impact of complex environment. Several lanthanide-based RF probes have been developed to recognize DPA such as Tb-micelle (Luan et al., 2018), Tb-fluorapatite (Xu et al., 2017), Tb-CDs (Chen et al., 2015), Eu-ZnO (Zhou et al., 2017), and Eu-CDs (Rong et al., 2018). However, the above reported RF probes usually combines directly with DPA on the surface to expose the probes to the external environment, which may lead to photobleaching and premature leakage of the probes, thus reducing the photo-stability and detection sensitivity (Fan et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2019).

In the past few years, lanthanide-metal organic frameworks (Ln3+-MOFs) have aroused extensive attention in the field of biological and environmental fluorescence sensing because of its dual advantages of Ln3+ and MOFs (Dong et al., 2016; Lian et al., 2017; Qin and Yan, 2018; Wu et al., 2019), including rich active sites of MOFs (Qiu et al., 2019a; Qiu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2016), unique fluorescence emission spectra of Ln3+ (Dong et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2018), and organic ligands that can transfer energy to Ln3+ by virtue of “antenna effect” (Lian et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2019). Presently, Ln-MOFs hybridized with CDs have been reported in the field of fluorescence sensing, but the number is still very limited (Hao et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2018; Xu and Yan, 2016). CDs doped Ln3+-MOFs RF probes have more active sites than the above reported RF probes, which can better chelate a variety of lanthanide ions (Ln3+) (Cui et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2018). More importantly, RF probe with “turn-on” mode constructed by Ln-MOFs can not only eliminate the man-made interference during the fluorescence detection, but also effectively avoid the false response and improve the signal-to-noise ratio, which provides a promising prospect for environmental and biological monitoring. Interestingly, compared with the classical colloidal gold method, our designed method for detecting Bacillus anthracis has the advantages of sharper color change and shorter detection time. We believe that there will be more and more research results based on the Ln-MOFs RF probe applied to in vivo monitoring, environmental sensing, drug delivery and imaging in the future.

In this paper, a one-pot in situ fabrication of method was proposed to embed CDs into Eu-MOFs cages for RF identification of CaDPA. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that CDs@Eu-MOF has been used to construct a RF platform to detect the anthrax biomarker CaDPA. In addition, the prepared CaDPA test strip with easy and convenient to carry and use is not interfered by other interference ligands.

Urea (57-13-6, 99.0%) and citric acid (5949-29-1, 99.5%) are purchased from Sinopharm Shenyang Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; Anhydrous sodium acetate (127-09-3, 99.0%) and absolute ethanol (64-17-5, 99.5%) are obtained from Shenyang Laibo Electronics Co., Ltd.; 1,3,5-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) (554-95-0, 98.0%) and 2,6-pyridine dicarboxylic acid (DPA) (499-83-2, 99.0%) are purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd.; Eu (NO)3·6H2O (10031-53-5, 99.99%) is obtained from Shandong Desheng New Materials Co., Ltd. All the experimental solutions were prepared in ultra-pure water, and the chemical regents used were analytical grade.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrometer (Varian Cary Eclipse 300, USA). The morphology of CDs@Eu-MOFs was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Semerfly TECNAI G2 F30, USA), and field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8000, Japan). The crystal structure of CDs@Eu-MOFs was performed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) and Cu kα radiation. The surface composition of CDs@Eu-MOFs was analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos axis supra, USA).

First, CDs were prepared and purified according to the previous reported (Tian et al., 2017). Specifically, 1.0 g citric acid and 2.0 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water by ultrasound. Then, the mixed homogeneous solution was placed in a 50 mL Teflon reactor, heated at 160 °C for 4 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained CDs aqueous solution was added to 20 mL of absolute ethanol and purified by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for three times. Finally, the solid CDs was obtained after drying at 60 °C for 12 h.

Subsequently, 410 mg of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, 90 mg of CDs, and 150 mg of anhydrous sodium acetate were ultrasonically dissolved in 45 mL of deionized water to obtain a solution A. 200 mg of benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) was sonicated in 45 mL ethanol to obtain a solution B. Immediately thereafter, the solution A was added to the solution B, and after vigorously stirring at room temperature for 60 min, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with ethanol and water, and then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h. The preparation method of Eu-MOFs is the same as the above steps, except that no CDs are added. The synthetic process of RF probe was illustrated in Scheme 1 .

45 mg of CDs@Eu-MOFs was dissolved in 50 mL 0.02 mol/L HEPES buffer solution with pH = 7.4 and disposed into a suspension with concentration of 0.9 mg/mL. A new 500 μL suspension of CDs@Eu-MOFs was added to a quartz cuvette. 300 μL CaDPA aqueous solutions with different concentrations (80, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1300, 1500, and 1700 μg/L) were added to the samples in turn to ensure that the total volume of the mixed solution was 3 mL. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after 25 s of mixing.

In order to evaluate the practical usability of the RF probes, tap water and human serum were selected for testing. The blood samples of healthy people were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, and the upper pale yellow transparent liquid was carefully drawn with a pipette for later use. 300 μL different concentrations (100, 500, 1000, and 1500 μg/L) of CaDPA were added to the real samples, and the fluorescence emission spectra of the actual samples were recorded. Among them, each sample was tested at least three times, and the average was taken for further analysis.

The morphology of the synthesized Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1A, B) and CDs@Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1C) was observed by SEM (Fig. 1). Fig. 1A and B are SEM images of Eu-MOFs, which show that the straw bundle-like structure of the synthesized Eu-MOFs is composed of thin micro-rods with a length and width of about 2-3 μm and 200–250 nm, respectively. When CDs were encapsulated into Eu-MOFs cage, the morphology of CDs@Eu-MOFs changed significantly, and the size of CDs@Eu-MOFs decreased obviously (Fig. 1C). It can be concluded that CDs may tailor the morphology of Eu-MOFs crystals. The reason for regulating the morphology may be that there are abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, which form competitive coordination of carboxyl groups from various sources and inhibit the growth of nanocrystals. Further enlarged SEM images show that the length and diameter of microrods are in the range of 1–2 μm and 100–120 nm, respectively (Fig. 1C). Moreover, different scales of SEM images were also used to further demonstrate the changes in the surface morphology of Eu-MOFs before and after chelated CDs (Fig. S1).

The morphologies of CDs (Fig. 1D) and CDs@Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1E and F) were characterized by TEM. TEM of the CDs shows that the particle size of CDs is about 3–4 nm. TEM found seemingly that each nanorod is made up of sheets that are stacked tightly. At the same time, no CDs were observed on the surface of CDs@Eu-MOFs, which may be due to the very small size of CDs entering the interior of Eu-MOFs crystal.

The synthesized Eu-MOFs before and after the addition of the CDs were structurally characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 2A. Comparing the peaks of the synthesized products with the simulated peaks, it can be seen that the peaks of the samples are completely consistent with the simulated peaks (CCDC: 291770), and the strong diffraction peaks intensity indicate that the samples have higher crystallinity. The doped CDs does not affect the crystal structure, which may be due to the very small size of the CDs and the internal embedding of the Eu-MOFs.

To further prove the successful preparation of CDs@Eu-MOFs, the elemental compositions of CDs@Eu-MOFs (curve a), Eu-MOFs (curve b), and CDs (curve c) were investigated by XPS (Fig. 2B), and the high-resolution spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs were also analyzed. Compared with Eu-MOFs, the surface of CDs@Eu-MOFs not only has the same Eu, C and O as Eu-MOFs, but also has characteristic peak N derived from CDs surface (Fig. 2B). Fig. 2C revealed that CDs@Eu-MOFs contains (SP3) CN and (SP3) NH two characteristic peaks, while Eu-MOFs does not contain the characteristic peaks of N (Fig. 2D), which further indicates that CDs@Eu-MOFs has been successfully prepared. Fig. 2E and F demonstrated that the Eu valence states derived from Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs are mainly Eu3+ (Eu3+ 3d3/2 and 3d5/2) (Alammar et al., 2018). Moreover, elemental analysis results of the CDs@Eu-MOFs observed by XPS was exhibited in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of RF probe to CaDPA
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3A, the obtained CDs@Eu-MOFs RF probe has an emission peak attributed to CDs at 455 nm, which further confirms that the CDs@Eu-MOFs have been successfully synthesized. After adding 170 μg/L DPA, there were three strong emission peaks at 592 nm, 616 nm and 693 nm induced by the characteristic emission of Eu3+ at excitation wavelengths of 280 nm (Fig. 3A(b), and B (b)), and the strongest emission of 616 nm was attributed to the 5D0 → 7F2 level transition of Eu3+ that located in the red region. However, in this system, CDs play the role of a good internal standard fluorescent material, and CaDPA cannot quench the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 3A, and C). What's more interesting is that when the concentration of CaDPA added to RF probe is 170 μg/L, the color change can be visually recognized by ultraviolet lamp (Inset of Fig. 3A), so as to achieve effective visual detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response time of RF probe to CaDPA
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Fig. 3D that the fluorescence of the system is weak without CaDPA. After adding different concentrations (15, 80, and 140 μg/L) CaDPA, the fluorescence of the system increased rapidly, reached a stable level for 25 s and remained stable for at least 15 min. The results show that the combination of CaDPA and Eu3+ is very rapid, which provides a powerful means for on-line monitoring CaDPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CaDPA detection based on a turn on approach
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized experimental conditions, the intrinsic fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 616 nm gradually enhanced with the increase of CaDPA concentration, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not change (Fig. 4A). As shown in Fig. 4B, the ratio of the intensity values (I616/I455) is linearly fitted to the CaDPA concentration in the range of 8–170 μg/L (R = 0.9990), the detection limit (3σ/k) is 0.66 μg/L (n = 9), and the precision of the method is 3.1% (n = 11, 100 μg/L). To compare the sensitivity of the probes, CaDPA was detected using a single emission fluorescent probe Eu-MOFs. As was shown in Fig. 4C and D, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs at 616 nm increased with the increase of DPA concentration, and showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of CaDPA between 12 and 170 μg/L. The correlation coefficient was 0.9983, the detection limit (3σ/k) was 1.09 μg/L (n = 9), and the precision of the method was 3.8% (n = 11, 100 μg/L). Obviously, the CDs@Eu-MOFs RF probe has higher sensitivity for CaDPA detection compared to the Eu-MOFs single emission sensor.

The reason for the increase in fluorescence may be due to CaDPA is a tridentate chelating ligand with two carboxyl groups, and a pyridinium nitrogen as a coordination site, which can have stronger metal ligand chelation coordination and cation-π interaction with Eu3+ (Salvia et al., 2015), resulting in energy transfer from CaDPA to Eu3+, which achieves Eu3+ luminescence enhancement (Gui et al., 2019) (Scheme 1).

Selectivity is an important parameter to further evaluate the performance of a novel biosensor. Therefore, the response of RF sensor to a variety of potentially interfering substances have been investigated, including common metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cl−, NO3 −, and CO3 2−), amino acids (l-Phenylalanine, d-aspartic, glutamine, methionine, l-cysteine, and glycine (l-Phe, Asp, Glu, Met, l-Cys, and Gly)), and aromatic compounds (benzoic acid, o-phthalic acid, m-phthalic acid (BA, o-PA, and m-PA)). The concentration of all the potential interferences was 300 μg/L. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the signal response and color of the CDs@Eu-MOFs sensor can hardly be influenced by various potentially interfering substances. After the addition of 150 μg/L CaDPA, significant fluorescence enhancement and color change are produced. The results show that CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibit a high fluorescence selective response toward the detection of anthrax spore biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CaDPA in water and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the feasibility of CDs@Eu-MOFs RF probes in environmental and biological determination, the ability of RF probes to recognize CaDPA in tap water and human serum samples were investigated. The undiluted tap water samples and diluted 60-fold serum samples were assayed by standard addition method, and 10, 50, 100, and 150 μg/L CaDPA standard solutions were added to the solution for RF test. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery was between 94.0 and 104.7%, indicating that the RF probe used to detect CaDPA in tap water and human serum samples is accurate and reliable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CaDPA on test strips
ce:section.ce:para: Because the constructed RF probe has excellent “turn on” property for CaDPA, a series of test strip with the ability to detect DPA on-line have been prepared. Briefly, the test strip was prepared by spraying a CDs@Eu-MOFs probe solution on the surface of a note paper that does not produce fluorescence and then drying. In addition, the surface of the test strip is sprayed with an analyte solution (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, l-Phe, Asp, Glu, Met, l-Cys, Gly, BA, o-PA, m-PA, and DPA). As shown in Fig. 6A, only the test strip coated with CaDPA solution emits red fluorescence under the irradiation of an UV lamp, while the test strip sprayed with other analyte solution (170 μg/L) emits only blue fluorescence, and the color change can be recognized by our naked eyes. Then, the CaDPA solution was served as the ink, and the pre-treated paper strip was used as the test strip. The letter “CaDPA” written by CaDPA exhibits red luminescence under illumination by an UV lamp (Inset Fig. 6B(a)). More interestingly, such inks are not interfered with some common mixed amino acids (Inset Fig. 6B(b)) and a few mixed aromatic carboxylic acids with a structure similar to CaDPA (Inset Fig. 6B(c)). The research results show that the RF probe has potential as a test strip for the detection of anthrax spore biomarkers on-line, and displays potential application value in the field of environmental and biological monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic gate device based on “turn-on” behavior
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, the logic gate strategy that CDs@Eu-MOFs can be used to monitor CaDPA concentration levels is also demonstrated. Firstly, the simplest one-to-one decoder is designed based on the interaction between CDs@Eu-MOFs and CaDPA. Regarding the input, the presence and absence of CaDPA is defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of F616 and F455 is taken as the output signal with a threshold of 0.2, respectively. In the absence of CaDPA, an output (0, 0) is produced. After contacting CaDPA, an output (1, 1) is generated. Fig. 7A, B, and C show the column bars of the normalized fluorescence intensity, truth value table of the one-to-one decoder logic gate, and truth table of the logic platform for different concentrations of CaDPA monitoring. The fabricated logical operations based on RF changes can perform well in detecting CaDPA.

In summary, a RF probe for detection of anthrax biomarker was developed using a one-pot method to embed CDs into Eu-MOFs cage. The constructed self-calibrating RF probe can respond quickly to CaDPA, and exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity. The fluorescence response mechanism was also discussed in detail. The tunable luminesce color from blue to red allows CDs@Eu-MOFs to be used as sensitive colorimetric luminescence sensors. The test strip experiment proves that the RF probe has practicality for on-site detection of CaDPA. Moreover, an INHIBIT logic gate based on CaDPA monitoring is developed. Therefore, the method reported in this contribution can provide a new strategy for the rapid and sensitive Bacillus anthracis spore sensing, which has important practical significance for environmental analysis and biological monitoring.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Kuiyu Yi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Xiaoting Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis. Lei Zhang: Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51672116), Liaoning Province Science and Technology Planning Project (No. 20180551203), the Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Project for Innovative Talent Training (No. LT2017010). The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.140692.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ai et al., 2009

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Europium-based fluorescence nanoparticle sensor for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ai, K., Zhang, B., Lu, L., 2009. Europium-based fluorescence nanoparticle sensor for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker. Angew. Chem. Int. Edit. 48, 304–308. doi:10.1002/anie.200804231.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence properties of mechanochemically synthesized lanthanide containing MIL-78 MOFs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alammar, T., Hlova, I.Z., Gupta, S., Balema, V., Pecharsky, V.K., Mudring, A.V., 2018. Luminescence properties of mechanochemically synthesized lanthanide containing MIL-78 MOFs. Dalton T. 47, 7594–7601. doi:10.1039/C7DT04771A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Aspholm et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biochemical and mutational analysis of spore cortex-lytic enzymes in the food spoiler Bacillus licheniformis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aspholm, M.E., Kollerud, K.K., Hansen, H.C.H., Granum, P.E., Christie, G., Lindbäck, T., 2019. Biochemical and mutational analysis of spore cortex-lytic enzymes in the food spoiler Bacillus licheniformis. Food Microbiol. 84, 103259–103267. doi:10.1016/j.fm.2019.103259.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Beverly and Voorhees, 2000

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron monochromator mass spectrometry for the analysis of whole bacteria and bacterial spores
